8: Six-Part Symmetries I have found only one clear example of a psalm arranged in a six-part chiasmus. This structural scheme, like the four-part chiasmus, offers no central position of emphasis but allows for the reiteration of important points through the repetition in matching units. Psalm 115 The six-part structure of Psalm 115 draws out the contrast between Yahweh, the God who protects and helps Israel, and the useless idols of the pagan nations a introductory call to praise (115:1) b nations' taunt: where is Israel's God? (115:2-3) answer: "our God is in the heavens!" c pagans trust in useless idols that can do nothing (115:4-8) the folly of all those who trust (bth) in them c' let Israel trust in Yahweh, its help and shield! (115:9-11) " repeated exhortation to trust (bth) In Yahweh b' response to taunt: Yahweh is creator of heaven and earth; may he bless Israeli (115:12-16) o "the heavens are Yahweh's heavens!" a' concluding call to praise (115:17-18) (David A. Dorsey, The Literary Structure of the Old Testament)
